title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyou Instructional Program Review
Administration of Justice Program-Academic Degree Education (A.A., A.S.-T., C.O.A.)
Leigh Moore (Preparer and Contributor)
Program Description:
The Administration of Justice (ADJ) program consists of an Associate of Science (A.S.)
degree, Associate of Science Degree for Transfer (A.S.-T.), and an 18-unit Certificate of
Achievement (C.O.A.).
This program introduces students to the study of the criminal justice system in the
United States. It offers students preparation for careers in a variety of criminal justice
fields at the local, state, and federal levels, as well as Tribal judicial services.
Coursework also prepares students interested in pursuing careers in the legal
profession.
The Administration of Justice Program also offers a comprehensive Public Safety
Training Program including a Modular Law Enforcement Academy and State-mandated
training courses for the Law Enforcement Community.
Further, the Administration of Justice Program offers a Standards and Training in
Corrections (STC) approved Juvenile Correctional Officer to Deputy Probation Officer
Transfer Academy, twice per year.
a) Organization: The ADJ Program is under the direction and supervision of
the Dean of Career and Technical Education. The ADJ faculty includes
one full-time Faculty Instructor in the degree curriculum. In this past review
cycle, one adjunct instructor was assigned to the program.
b) Primary purpose: The primary purpose of the program is to prepare
students for careers in criminal justice and related fields. The A.S. and
A.S.-T. also prepares students for further academic study in the legal field.
Students receiving an A.S. or A.S.-T also are prepared to work in a variety
of social service fields which are in contact with the justice system.
c) Whom you serve: We serve all students interested in seeking a degree in
Administration of Justice, completing coursework which meets General
Education requirements, or students interested in the subject matter to
enhance their knowledge of the justice profession. Courses offer an
emphasis for those interested in criminal justice careers, as well as
students seeking education in law and social services which involve court
intervention. Some students are presently employed as criminal justice
professionals, and are seeking additional education to improve their
knowledge and skills, which increases their opportunity for advancement
in their agency.

-- 1 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 2)
text:
Students in the Administration of Justice program live not only in our
immediate service area (Siskiyou County), but from all over California and
neighboring states, through our online courses.
d) Services provided: We offer curriculum leading to
• Associate of Arts, Associate of Science degree,
• Associate of Science for Transfer degree, and a
• Certificate of Achievement.
• Opportunities for internships with local agencies through our
Occupational Work Experience Program have been explored,
although no students have currently been enrolled in the last review
cycle. Consideration is given to students entering courses with
limited college-work experience.
e) How are services provided: Students are encouraged to meet with the
Academic Advising/Counseling office prior to enrollment, and to continue
to utilize guidance through that office throughout their academic career.
Students are referred to the Academic Success Center when deemed
appropriate, and are given additional academic support from the instructor
if skills are needed to successfully complete the course.
f) Courses incorporate the latest in research and industry standards. This
includes research within the industry which incorporates evidence-based
evaluations and incorporates scientific methods to address issues in the
criminal justice system.
During the last curriculum revision process, three courses were approved
for transfer to the University of California. Courses are reviewed every two
years and updated to match C-ID description, appropriate objectives, and
detailed student learning outcomes. Seven of the eight current course
offerings per year are transferable to the University of California system,
and all eight are transferrable to the California State University system.
With the implementation of Cal-GETC, it is anticipated that the upcoming
update will ensure courses remain transferrable, and those counted under
general education will be reapproved.
The ADJ faculty member responsible for transfer coursework is a member
of the Student Learning Outcome (SLO) Committee, including ongoing
standards and evaluation regarding learning outcomes.
Courses required for the degree are offered on a Fall and Spring
Semester cycle. Courses on the following cycle:

-- 2 of 16 --

FALL SPRING
ADJ 1001 Introduction to Criminal Justice ADJ 1001 Introduction to Criminal Justice
ADJ 2002 Concepts of Criminal Law ADJ 1517 Community and the Justice
System
ADJ 1503 Legal Aspects of Evidence ADJ 1505 Criminal Investigation
ADJ 2528 Criminology (Elective) ADJ 2524 Introduction to Corrections
ADJ 2522, California Statutory Law (Spring offering), and ADJ 2525, Criminal Court
Process (Fall offering), were offered in the latest program review cycle each offering
cycle, although based on a lack of enrollment, the courses were cancelled.
The following is data regarding enrollment during the last review cycle:

-- 3 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 3)
text:
Analysis: As seen, there is higher enrollment in Fall courses, with a drop in Spring
enrollments. Some of this could be attributed to the ADJ 1001, which qualifies as an
Area 4 Social Sciences general education course (UC) and first-time students are
enrolled in the course for that reason.
External Factors with Significant Impact:
a) Budgetary Constraints or opportunities:
Budgetary Constraints: Not specifically noted.
Opportunities: Introducing cadets currently enrolled in the Modular Police
Academy to information regarding completion of a degree in Administration of
Justice would be desirable. Students could be provided with information
regarding transfer requirements, and the benefit of advanced education in
promotion opportunities as they progress through their career.
b) Competition from other institutions: In the North Far North region, several
community colleges within districts with higher populations exist: Butte
College (Chico, CA.), Shasta College (Redding, CA), and College of the
Redwoods (Eureka, CA), all have degree programs.
c) The current A.S.-T. aligns with transfer to the California State University,
Sacramento. In the last review cycle, an emphasis has been placed on
making coursework transferrable to the University of California system.
d) Institutional regulation, policies, mandates: Recruitment and outreach during
the last review cycle presented some overlapping issues with other staff
taking the lead on recruitment.
e) Non-Institutional regulation, policies, mandates: There is an opportunity for
growth with dual enrollment through area high schools. However, some area
high schools readily accept college coursework as meeting Career Technical
Education toward high school credits, while others do not. College of the
Siskiyous intends to hire a Dual Enrollment Coordinator/Counselor who may
be able to work with area high schools in finding a method to allow increased

-- 4 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 4)
text:
enrollment through local high schools (see high school enrollment numbers in
graph below).
f) Job market:
• Requirement of prospective employers: The Administration of
Justice degree includes students wishing to attend a POST
academy, but also are students seeking work in corrections, law,
social work, and other related fields. The requirements for different
peace officer positions in California vary depending on the agency
and the specific peace officer duties. For example, probation
officers are normally required to have a Bachelor’s Degree or an
Associate’s Degree. However, correctional officers may only be
required to have basic training and certification through Standards
and Corrections (STC) Correctional CORE, but, advancement in a
particular agency may require more advanced coursework.
• Development: A significant development in the field of POST-
certified peace officers is that officers will be required to earn a
Modern Policing Degree, beginning with those hired on or after
November of 2025. College of the Siskiyous anticipates offering this
degree in Fall of 2025.
Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
a) Summarize progress on (Student Learning Outcome) SLO measures: Since the
last review cycle, COS has implemented a robust SLO assessment effort,
including the formation of an SLO Committee, in which the ADJ faculty member
is a committee member.
In the Fall of 2023, the course with consistently the highest enrollment, ADJ 1001
was assessed, and showed that students who remained in the course and
engaged in coursework overwhelmingly met or exceeded SLOs for that course.
There were three students who stopped engaging in the course but could not be
dropped (based on the point in the course where dropping was not an option),
and as a result did not meet SLOs. Two students met some but not all of the
SLOs for the course, and this small sampling was the result of only marginal
engagement from the students. Therefore, it appears that SLOs are appropriately
met in ADJ 1001 when students complete the variety of learning activities
throughout the course.
In the Spring of 2024, ADJ 2524 was assessed. All students in that course met or
exceeded expectations in all SLOs identified. The sampling was smaller, but one
notable fact is the students taking ADJ 2524 tend to identify Administration of
Justice as their major. This data is consistent with the last review cycle which
showed the same or similar results.
At the conclusion of the Fall 2024 semester, ADJ 1503 will be assessed.
b) Specific adjustments: Three courses were converted to ZTC in the Fall of 2024.
This created additional consideration to ensure that material provided ensured
effective learning for the course. Some modifications are made when a particular
SLO is showing concern.

-- 5 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 5)
text:
c) The plan within the next two-year cycle will be to complete SLO assessments on
all SLOs within at least one course each semester.
Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
Below is data on students who completed a degree for transfer. As considered below,
although ADJ is a CTE program and A.A./A.S.-T. might be the educational objective for
students, this is an area which needs improvement.
It will be the intent of this program to collaborate with the Advising and Counseling office
to increase student knowledge regarding transfer options, encourage students to
consider transfer to a four-year degree, and identify any supports needed to increase
this area of interest for students in the ADJ program.
Success Rate: There was a decline in successful completion of courses between Fall of
2022 and Fall of 2023. As previously mentioned, one course (ADJ 1001) is attempted
by a large array of students who are fulfilling general education requirements and may
or may not be majoring in ADJ. This is believed to impact success rates in this course.
In ADJ 1503 in Fall of 2023, the course was offered by an adjunct professor. The
professor used a supplemental program which accompanied the textbook, in order to
grade assessments.
Effective Fall of 2024, the supplemental program is no longer being used, and each
exam question is vetted. This was another course which was converted to ZTC and it is
the opinion of this author that accompanying test questions are particularly vague and
therefore, scrutiny of each test question is necessary prior to opting its use. It is
anticipated that all the students in ADJ 1503 in Fall of 2024 will successfully pass the
course.
The goal for success rates in all ADJ courses is 90% or higher. Although 100% success
is most desirable, for the reasons outlined regarding the ADJ 1001 course, this appears
to be an appropriate goal.

-- 6 of 16 --

Below (following page) are the success rates for ADJ courses for Fall 2022 and Fall
2023, and for Spring 2023 and Spring 2024:

-- 7 of 16 --

Data regarding full-time faculty to student ratio:
Other Unit Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research:
This will be addressed in Evaluation.
Evaluation:
In the following pages, data regarding student representation, including gender, race,
age of students, modality of coursework, and other data considerations are explored.
Student demographics are considered and focus on recruiting and engaging students in
underrepresented groups is a priority. Although there appears to not be a significant
gender disparity in the current measurements, racial disparities continue to exist, in
comparison to the service area’s overall demographic.
The author and faculty member of this report has engaged with community members,
particularly the large Indigenous population in Siskiyou County. While Native Americans
make up nearly 6% of the county’s population, data regarding their representation in the
ADJ courses is unknown, which is a significant concern. During the last review cycle,
only 1% of the student population was identified as Native American. Efforts to maintain
and further those partnerships are ongoing.

-- 8 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 6)
text:

Representation by age:
Modality of instruction. Up until Fall of 2024, all ADJ degree program coursework was
offered through distance learning, the result of a grant converting this to a fully online
degree offering. It is the intent of the program to consider student learning needs,
including offering additional modalities if viable. One course in the Fall of 2024 was
offered in-person, with a 100% success rate and no drops.

-- 11 of 16 --

-- 12 of 16 --

Below is a course which is listed in error, and should be identified as degree applicable,
and transferrable to both UC and CSU. As of the writing of this report, this has been
forwarded to the appropriate analysts for correction:
Student type (continuing, first-time, K-12, returning student):

-- 13 of 16 --

High school student enrollment (dual enrollment or concurrent enrollment):
Summary of Evaluation: Certain student groups remain underrepresented, and data is
inconclusive as to the severity of that gap. However, ongoing efforts are being made to
engage community members and increase partnerships.
Evidence-based practices in policing, corrections, and court practices is the focus of the
curriculum within the ADJ degree program. Students are actively engaged in research
and analysis as to the latest issues facing the criminal justice system, and utilizing
proven strategies and methods to address those issues.
In review of resources, the Regional Training Center adjacent to the Modular Academy
is an avenue for future course offerings, as well as engagement with community
stakeholders. For example, the Karuk Tribe has requested short-term justice courses for
their judicial staff. However, internal logistics has prevented from this being explored
further. It is anticipated that in the next review cycle, engagement and collaboration with

-- 14 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 7)
text:
justice partners such as the Karuk Tribe will allow the program to grow and meet the
needs of the community.
The ADJ program has one full-time faculty member. No administrative support is utilized
based on organizational structure at this time. However, there is opportunity to extend
outreach through current available staff (i.e. CTE coordinator).
Vision:
• Based on the passage of AB 89, which reforms the educational requirements for
peace officers who work in California and fall under the certification of Peace
Officers Standards and Training (POST), increased program interest, enrollment
and completion is expected in the next review cycle. Although not an ADJ
degree, some coursework in ADJ is required. Therefore, growth is expected.
• Collaboration with the Regional Training Center would be a significant
development for the ADJ and Academy Programs. Short-term training courses
that are certified by Standards and Training in Corrections (STC) and POST
could have a significant and positive impact on the programs and translate to
increased enrollment in ADJ degree courses. It would also benefit industry
partners and the community at large.
• Continue to engage community stakeholders, including those who are
underrepresented in the criminal justice field.
• Engage in discussions and increase collaboration with area high schools for dual
enrollment opportunities. CTE requirements in high school could be a viable
pathway to increased interest not only in the industry but also in completing
college coursework while in high school.
Progress of Prior Goals:
• Many of the courses slated for review and possible approval for UC transferability
were approved during the last review cycle. One additional course will be
submitted for UC transfer (ADJ 2002), and all courses are up for mandatory
revision in March (2025). During that time, the revisions will be made to ensure
ADJ 1001 remains an Area 4 general education course.
• As mentioned previously, outreach with the community, including the Karuk
Tribe, was achieved, however, further efforts will be a priority. Effective and
equitable representation of our community continues to be a focus for the ADJ
program.
Continuous Quality Improvement:
Vision of the program addresses several areas of the intent for continuous quality
improvement. Priorities for the coming review cycle:
1. Submit the Modern Policing Degree (AB 89 requirement) to the Chancellor’s
Office, for targeted offering in the Fall of 2025.
2. Engage educational stakeholders such as local area high schools, to explore
possible dual enrollment options for high school students.

-- 15 of 16 --

title: ADJ_Program_Review_Academic Program_2023-2024 (Part 8)
text:
3. Ensure that ADJ courses continue to be transferable to the UC system, and
maintain continuity with the Cal-GETC transition.
4. Engage in ongoing recruitment efforts.
5. Increase student success through measured assessments and redesign of
activities, assessments, and exams to ensure maximum success.
Closing the loop:
Although a CQIPs was submitted in the Spring of 2023 for library resources to meet the
needs of students unable to purchase a textbook, that submission fell off the IPB
agenda, based on a determination it was a low-priority item. No other Resource
Allocation Request (RAR) has been submitted from this program.
